name: Microsoft Entra ID Security Configuration
description: Securing identities in Microsoft Entra ID using MFA, Conditional Access, and Identity Protection.
key_facts: Entra ID Identity Protection detects risky users and risky sign-ins using machine learning models
Risk-based Conditional Access policies can require MFA or block access for high-risk sign-ins
Named locations define trusted IP ranges; sign-ins from unknown locations increase risk scores
Entra ID Password Protection blocks weak and banned passwords including custom banned lists
Authentication methods policies control which MFA methods users can register and use
common_misconceptions: Identity Protection risk detections are signals, not guarantees; some may be false positives
Security Defaults are free but less customizable than Conditional Access; cannot coexist with CA policies

name: Privileged Identity Management and Privileged Access
description: Managing and securing privileged access to Azure AD and Azure resources using PIM and PAW.
key_facts: PIM enforces JIT access for privileged roles requiring activation with MFA and justification
Access reviews in PIM periodically validate that role assignments are still needed
Privileged Access Workstations (PAW) provide isolated devices for admin tasks to reduce attack surface
Azure AD roles managed by PIM include Global Admin, Security Admin, User Admin, and custom roles
PIM alerts detect standing privilege, excessive admin counts, and other risky configurations
common_misconceptions: PIM eligible assignments do not grant active access; users must activate the role before it is effective
PIM access reviews are separate from Entra ID Governance access reviews; both must be configured

name: Azure RBAC Advanced Configuration
description: Designing and implementing custom RBAC roles and managing access at scale across Azure subscriptions.
key_facts: Custom roles define specific permitted actions using Actions, NotActions, DataActions, and NotDataActions
Deny assignments are created by Azure Blueprints and managed apps; cannot be created manually by users
Conditions in role assignments restrict access based on resource attributes (ABAC)
Management Group RBAC assignments inherit down through subscriptions and resource groups
Just Enough Access: audit role assignments regularly to remove unused permissions
common_misconceptions: NotActions do not explicitly deny; they only remove from Actions; Deny assignments override NotActions
DataActions control data plane operations (e.g., read blob data) separate from management plane Actions

name: Azure Firewall and Network Security
description: Deploying and managing Azure Firewall for centralized network security and traffic inspection.
key_facts: Azure Firewall is a stateful managed network security service with high availability and scale
Firewall Policy organizes rules into Rule Collection Groups with priorities from 100-65000
DNAT rules translate inbound public IPs to private IPs; SNAT masquerades outbound private IPs
Threat Intelligence integration blocks traffic from known malicious IPs and FQDNs
Azure Firewall Premium adds IDPS (signature-based), TLS inspection, and URL filtering
common_misconceptions: Azure Firewall Premium IDPS operates in alert mode by default; Deny mode requires explicit configuration
User-Defined Routes must force traffic through Azure Firewall; simply deploying it does not redirect traffic

name: Azure DDoS Protection and Web Application Firewall
description: Defending Azure resources from volumetric attacks and web application vulnerabilities.
key_facts: DDoS Protection Basic is free and enabled by default; Standard provides adaptive tuning and telemetry
DDoS Standard provides per-resource protection policies auto-tuned based on traffic patterns
Azure WAF on Application Gateway protects web apps with OWASP Core Rule Sets (CRS 3.2+)
Azure WAF on Front Door provides global WAF protection at the edge for multi-region deployments
WAF modes: Detection (logs only) and Prevention (logs and blocks matched requests)
common_misconceptions: DDoS Protection Standard is per VNet, not per resource; all public IPs in the VNet benefit from it
WAF CRS rules protect against OWASP Top 10; custom rules address organization-specific requirements

name: Azure Private Endpoints and Private Link
description: Securing PaaS service access using private network connectivity to eliminate public internet exposure.
key_facts: Private Endpoint assigns a private IP from your VNet to a PaaS service like Storage or SQL Database
Azure Private Link is the underlying technology enabling private endpoints
DNS must be configured to resolve the service FQDN to the private IP instead of the public IP
Private endpoints prevent data exfiltration by restricting traffic to specific service instances
Network policies can be applied to private endpoint subnet for additional NSG control
common_misconceptions: Service endpoints and private endpoints are different; service endpoints route traffic over Azure backbone but the service still has a public IP
Private endpoints require DNS configuration; without it, clients may still resolve to the public IP

name: Microsoft Defender for Cloud
description: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) and Cloud Workload Protection Platform (CWPP) for Azure.
key_facts: Defender for Cloud provides Secure Score, Recommendations, and Alerts across Azure and multi-cloud
CSPM (free tier): continuous assessment and security recommendations without threat detection
Defender plans add CWPP capabilities for VMs, SQL, Storage, Kubernetes, App Service, and more
Security benchmarks like MCSB (Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark) define compliance baselines
Workflow automation triggers Logic Apps on alerts or recommendations for automated remediation
common_misconceptions: Defender for Cloud Secure Score reflects configuration posture, not actual threat activity
Enabling all Defender plans incurs per-resource costs; evaluate which plans are needed for your environment

name: Microsoft Sentinel SIEM and SOAR
description: Deploying and operating Microsoft Sentinel for threat detection, investigation, and automated response.
key_facts: Sentinel Analytics rules define detection logic using KQL queries with scheduling and thresholds
Scheduled Analytics rules run KQL on Log Analytics at defined intervals; NRT rules run near-real-time
Incidents aggregate related alerts into a single investigation unit with entity mapping
Hunting queries allow proactive threat search using KQL without automated alert generation
UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics) baselines normal behavior to detect anomalies
common_misconceptions: Sentinel incidents are not the same as alerts; alerts are detection results, incidents group alerts for investigation
UEBA requires enabling and a data collection period before producing meaningful behavior baselines

name: Azure Key Vault Advanced Security
description: Implementing comprehensive secret, key, and certificate management with Key Vault for enterprise security.
key_facts: Key Vault access is controlled by Azure RBAC (recommended) or vault access policies (legacy)
Soft-delete protects keys, secrets, and certificates from accidental deletion with a configurable retention period
Purge protection prevents purging deleted objects before the retention period ends
Key Vault HSM (Premium SKU) stores keys in FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated HSMs
Private endpoints should be used for Key Vault in production to prevent public access
common_misconceptions: Access policies and RBAC on Key Vault serve the same purpose but differently; RBAC is more granular
Soft-delete is enabled by default on new vaults; purge protection must be explicitly enabled

name: Azure Storage Security
description: Implementing security controls for Azure Storage including encryption, network access, and identity-based authentication.
key_facts: Storage accounts encrypt all data at rest with Microsoft-managed or customer-managed keys
Shared Key access can be disabled; Azure RBAC and Entra ID data roles should be preferred
Network rules restrict access to specific VNets, IP ranges, or trusted Microsoft services
Defender for Storage detects unusual data access patterns and potential malware uploads
Immutable Blob Storage with WORM (Write Once Read Many) policy prevents blob modification for compliance
common_misconceptions: HTTPS enforcement at storage account level prevents HTTP access but does not enforce encryption at rest
Customer-managed keys (CMK) require Key Vault and increase operational responsibility vs Microsoft-managed keys

name: Azure SQL Database Security
description: Securing Azure SQL Database with authentication, encryption, auditing, and threat protection.
key_facts: Azure SQL supports SQL authentication and Entra ID authentication (Windows Auth equivalent)
Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts database files at rest automatically
Always Encrypted protects sensitive columns with client-side encryption; server never sees plaintext
SQL Auditing logs all database events to Log Analytics, Storage, or Event Hubs
Defender for SQL detects SQL injection, unusual access patterns, and brute force attacks
common_misconceptions: TDE and Always Encrypted are complementary; TDE protects files at rest, Always Encrypted protects column data from DBAs
SQL Auditing generates large volumes of data; filter audit actions to essential events to control costs

name: Azure Policy and Security Governance
description: Using Azure Policy to enforce security standards, remediate configurations, and maintain compliance at scale.
key_facts: Security policies in Defender for Cloud are backed by Azure Policy assignments
DeployIfNotExists policies can auto-deploy security agents (MMA, Defender extension) to VMs
Policy exemptions allow specific resources to be excluded from policy evaluation with justification
Regulatory compliance dashboards in Defender for Cloud map policies to compliance frameworks
Azure Security Benchmark maps 90+ controls to Azure policy definitions for easy compliance implementation
common_misconceptions: Policy compliance percentage reflects how many resources pass; 100% does not mean no vulnerabilities
Policy remediation tasks fix existing non-compliant resources; new resources are handled at deployment

name: Azure VM Security and Compute Hardening
description: Securing Azure Virtual Machines against vulnerabilities and attacks using built-in and third-party tools.
key_facts: Defender for Servers (Plan 2) includes Defender for Endpoint, vulnerability assessment, and JIT VM Access
Just-in-Time VM Access temporarily opens management ports (RDP/SSH) only when needed
Azure Bastion eliminates direct public RDP/SSH exposure using browser-based secure access
Update Manager keeps OS patches current; auto-patching can be enabled per VM
Disk encryption at rest uses Azure Disk Encryption (BitLocker/DM-Crypt) or Server-side Encryption with CMK
common_misconceptions: JIT VM Access requires Defender for Servers Plan 2; it is not available in the free tier
Azure Bastion and JIT are complementary; Bastion removes public RDP/SSH, JIT controls who and when

name: Azure Monitor and Security Log Management
description: Collecting, storing, and analyzing security logs using Azure Monitor and Log Analytics for threat hunting.
key_facts: Log Analytics workspace is the central store for security logs from Azure resources and on-premises
Activity Log captures control plane operations (who did what to which resource and when)
Resource diagnostic logs capture data plane operations; must be explicitly configured per resource
Azure Monitor Alerts can trigger on security-relevant log conditions using KQL queries
Log retention in Log Analytics: default 30 days free, up to 730 days billable, archive up to 12 years
common_misconceptions: Activity Log is enabled by default but retention is only 90 days; archive to storage for longer retention
Diagnostic settings per resource must be configured; they do not automatically flow to Log Analytics

name: Application Security in Azure
description: Securing Azure applications with App Service security features, API protection, and secret management.
key_facts: App Service Authentication (Easy Auth) provides built-in identity provider integration without code changes
APIM policies enforce JWT validation, IP filtering, and rate limiting at the API gateway level
Microsoft Defender for App Service detects web shell injection, suspicious file access, and OWASP attacks
VNet integration for App Service routes outbound traffic through a VNet for private backend access
App Service Certificates manage SSL/TLS certificates with auto-renewal from a managed CA
common_misconceptions: App Service Easy Auth does not encrypt data; it only handles authentication at the platform level
VNet integration routes outbound traffic from the app into the VNet; it is not inbound access restriction
